intro_text:
Wharariki Beach sits at the top-left corner of New Zealand's South Island, on the Tasman Sea just west of Cape Farewell, the island's northernmost point. It is in Golden Bay, in the Tasman District, and you do not drive onto it. The beach is reached only by a roughly 20-minute walking track from the end of Wharariki Road, about 4 kilometres from the small settlement of Puponga; the path runs through farm paddocks of the Puponga Farm Park and a strip of coastal forest before it drops down to the sand.

What stands offshore is the reason people walk in. The Archway Islands are a small group of four islands and sea stacks, eroded into arches by the Tasman swell, ranging from the edge of the beach itself at low tide out to roughly 250 metres offshore. They are the shapes most of the world has seen without knowing it: the Archway Islands, photographed from a cave on this beach, are the default lock screen image of Microsoft's Windows 10. The photograph is widely attributed to Steve McCurry.

The beach is north-facing, flanked east and west by cliffs, and backed by large grassy dunes that are, in the words of the Wikipedia description, "quite exposed to winds." That exposure is not a footnote. Wharariki faces the open Tasman, and the wind that sculpts the dunes also throws spray and stings sand across the flats; the photographs that made the place famous are wind photographs — blur, spray, and bent marram grass. This is not a sheltered swimming bay but a wild edge of coast.

The other draw is the wildlife. Wharariki is known for a colony of New Zealand fur seals, and in summer the pups gather and play in the shallow rock pools, most reliably in the pools in front of the easternmost of the Archway Islands, reachable on foot at low tide. They are wild animals, not an exhibit: the Department of Conservation asks visitors to keep at least 20 metres back and not to follow or chase them, and harassing a seal is an offence under the Marine Mammals Protection Act 1978.

The water is the part the wallpaper does not show. The Department of Conservation states plainly that there are "strong currents and rips at Wharariki Beach, swimming not recommended." People come to walk the flats at low tide, watch the seals, and photograph the arches in the wind — not to swim. The name is older than all of it: wharariki is the Maori word for Phormium cookianum, the mountain flax of New Zealand's coastal cliffs.
spike_explainer:
The singular fact about Wharariki Beach is that you have almost certainly already seen it. The four sea stacks standing off the sand here, the **Archway Islands**, photographed looking out from inside a cave on the beach, are the **default lock screen image of Microsoft's Windows 10**. Windows 10 shipped in 2015 and ran on a billion-plus devices, which means this specific corner of Golden Bay became one of the most-viewed coastal images on Earth, glanced at by people who had no idea where it was, in the same accidental way the rolling green hill of the Windows XP "Bliss" wallpaper became famous a generation earlier. The photograph is **widely attributed to the photographer Steve McCurry** — the attribution is consistent across secondary sources, though Wikipedia states only that the islands are the default Windows 10 lock screen without naming the photographer, so treat the credit as well-reported rather than primary-sourced.

What the lock screen does not tell you is how hard this beach is to reach and how hostile the water is once you do. Wharariki is at the very top-left of the South Island, on the **Tasman Sea west of Cape Farewell**, the northernmost point of the South Island. There is no road to the sand. You park at the end of Wharariki Road, about **4 kilometres from the village of Puponga**, and walk a **roughly 20-minute track** that crosses the farm paddocks of the Puponga Farm Park and a band of coastal forest before descending to the beach. That walk-in is part of why the place stays wild: the crowd is self-selecting, filtered by a car park, a farm gate, and twenty minutes on foot.

The geography that makes the arches also makes the place exposed. Wharariki is **north-facing, flanked east and west by cliffs, and backed by large grassy dunes** that the standard description calls "quite exposed to winds." It faces the open Tasman with nothing upwind, so the prevailing wind drives spray off the surf and lifts sand off the dunes, and the famous images of Wharariki are almost all **wind images** — streaming sand, blurred spray, bent marram, low light raking across a wet beach. The beauty and the discomfort are the same weather. On a calm day it is a wide reflective flat; on a normal day it is a place where you lean into the wind and grit your teeth.

The **Archway Islands** themselves are a small archipelago of four islands and stacks, eroded by the Tasman swell into the arches they are named for. They range from being **part of the beach at low tide** — close enough to walk out toward — to roughly **250 metres offshore**. The tide is the clock here. At low water the beach opens up: the sand flats extend, the rock pools drain, and you can walk out across the wet sand toward the nearest stacks and into the pool systems at their feet. At high water that ground is gone and the beach narrows back against the cliffs. Timing a visit to low tide is the difference between seeing Wharariki and seeing a fraction of it.

The second reason to walk in is the **New Zealand fur seal** colony. In summer the pups haul out and gather to play in the shallow **rock pools**, and the reliable spot is the pools **in front of the easternmost of the Archway Islands**, accessible on foot at low tide. Watching seal pups tumble in a tidal pool is the kind of thing that reads as a zoo exhibit but is not one: these are wild animals on a wild coast. The **Department of Conservation** asks visitors to **keep at least 20 metres away** and not to follow or chase the seals, and harassment is an offence under the **Marine Mammals Protection Act 1978**. The pups are curious and will sometimes close the distance themselves; the rule is that you hold your ground or back off, you do not crowd them, and you keep dogs and small children clear.

Now the part the wallpaper crops out entirely: the water is genuinely dangerous and not for swimming. The Department of Conservation's own track page states that there are **"strong currents and rips at Wharariki Beach, swimming not recommended."** This is an unpatrolled, exposed Tasman Sea beach with no lifeguards, real surf, and rip currents running off the flats. The reanalysis wave record for this stretch of coast shows a **mean significant wave height around 1.2 metres year-round**, peaking near **1.4 metres in September** and with big-day waves over **2 metres**, on swells of roughly **7 to 8 seconds** — modest-sounding numbers that, combined with an open coast, cold water, strong rips, and no patrol, add up to water you look at rather than enter. People come to Wharariki to walk, to watch seals, and to photograph the arches in the wind. They do not come to swim, and the iconic image hides that completely.

That is the whole shape of the spike. Wharariki is one of the most-seen beaches in the world and one of the least-understood, because the version a billion people met was a still photograph: silent, weatherless, and empty. The real place is a 20-minute walk over a farm to a windblown, north-facing, rip-torn edge of the Tasman, where the famous arches are real but the swimming is genuinely unsafe, the seals are wild and legally protected, and the wind that makes the photographs is the same wind you have to stand in to take them. The name says as much in advance: **wharariki** is the Maori word for Phormium cookianum, the mountain flax that grows on exactly this kind of exposed coastal cliff. The beach was named for the tough plant that survives the wind here long before it was named, by accident, for a desktop.
honest_reckoning_note:
The image that made Wharariki famous is a lie of omission. The Windows 10 lock screen shows a silent, windless, empty beach; the real one is an unpatrolled, exposed Tasman Sea coast where the Department of Conservation states flatly that there are "strong currents and rips at Wharariki Beach, swimming not recommended." There are no lifeguards, the water is cold, the surf is real, and the rips run off the open flats. This is a place to walk and photograph, not to swim, and the wallpaper hides that entirely. Getting here at all means a roughly 20-minute walk from a car park 4 kilometres past Puponga, over farm paddocks and through coastal forest, into wind that throws sand and spray hard enough that the famous photographs are mostly wind photographs.

The seals are the other honest caveat. The fur seal pups in the rock pools are the draw, but they are wild animals on a wild coast, not an attraction laid on for visitors. The Department of Conservation asks people to keep at least 20 metres back and not to follow or chase them, and harassing a seal is an offence under the Marine Mammals Protection Act 1978. Pups are curious and will sometimes approach; the responsibility to keep distance sits with the human, and that means keeping dogs and small children well clear of the pools.
reckoning_pullquote: The version a billion people met was a still photograph: silent, weatherless, and empty — not a 20-minute walk over a farm to a rip-torn, windblown edge of the Tasman where swimming is flatly not recommended.

key_facts.label: Fame
key_facts.value: Archway Islands = default Windows 10 lock screen

key_facts.label: Photographer (attributed)
key_facts.value: Steve McCurry, shot from a cave on the beach
key_facts.source: Know Your Meme; secondary sources (not in Wikipedia)

key_facts.label: Location
key_facts.value: Tasman Sea, west of Cape Farewell (northernmost point of the South Island)

key_facts.label: Access
key_facts.value: ~20-minute walk over farmland from end of Wharariki Road; ~4 km from Puponga

key_facts.label: Archway Islands
key_facts.value: Four islands/stacks, from beach edge to ~250 m offshore

key_facts.label: Swimming
key_facts.value: Not recommended — strong currents and rips

key_facts.label: Seals
key_facts.value: Fur seal pups in rock pools; keep ≥20 m back (Marine Mammals Protection Act 1978)

key_facts.label: Name meaning
key_facts.value: Wharariki = Phormium cookianum, the mountain/coastal flax
key_facts.source: iNaturalist NZ; NZ native plant references

margin_notes.text: The Archway Islands shot from inside a beach cave is the Windows 10 default lock screen — one of the most-viewed coastal images on Earth, the way the XP 'Bliss' hill was a generation earlier. The credit to Steve McCurry is well-reported but not in Wikipedia.

The four Archway Islands are sea stacks eroded into arches by Tasman swell. They run from part of the beach at low tide out to roughly 250 metres offshore; the nearest are walkable on the flats at low water.
DOC's own words: 'strong currents and rips at Wharariki Beach, swimming not recommended.' Unpatrolled, cold, open Tasman coast. The wallpaper shows none of this. Come to walk and watch, not to swim.

Summer seal pups gather in the rock pools in front of the easternmost Archway Island. Keep at least 20 m back, don't follow or chase; harassment is an offence under the Marine Mammals Protection Act 1978.

Wharariki is the Maori name for Phormium cookianum, the mountain/coastal flax that grows on these exposed cliffs — the beach was named for the wind-hardy plant long before it was named, by accident, for a desktop.

No road reaches the sand. It's a ~20-minute walk from the end of Wharariki Road, about 4 km past Puponga, through Puponga Farm Park paddocks and coastal forest. The walk-in is what keeps the crowd thin.

day_in_time.dawn: First light over a near-empty beach, the 20-minute farm walk done in the cold before anyone else. If dawn lands near low tide the flats are wide and reflective and the seal pools in front of the eastern Archway Island are reachable; the wind is usually at its lightest of the day, which is the only time Wharariki is quiet.
day_in_time.midday: The busy window, and best timed to low tide so the sand flats open and you can walk out toward the nearest Archway stacks. Wind off the Tasman picks up through the day, throwing spray and sand; this is when the seal pools draw the most people, so keep the 20-metre distance from the pups even when others crowd in.
day_in_time.golden: Late sun rakes low across the wet sand and lights the arches from the side — the conditions that made the place famous. The wind that blurs spray and bends the marram grass is usually strongest now, which is exactly why the iconic Wharariki photographs look the way they do. Check the tide is still low enough to be out on the flats.
day_in_time.night: An unlit, unpatrolled, exposed coast with a 20-minute walk back over a dark farm track. There is nothing laid on here after dark and the rips don't switch off; the sensible move is to be back at the car park before the light goes.

food_drink.name: Nothing on the beach itself
food_drink.description: There is no food or drink service on the sand or at the trailhead. Wharariki is a walk-in conservation beach reached over farmland; bring water and anything you want to eat, and carry all rubbish back out. The nearest supplies are back toward Puponga and Collingwood.
food_drink.where: On the beach: none. Nearest provisions in Collingwood / Puponga, a drive away.

food_drink.name: Golden Bay / Collingwood cafe stop
food_drink.description: Collingwood, the last real village before the Wharariki Road turnoff, is the practical food stop before or after the walk — a small Golden Bay settlement with cafe and pub fare rather than anything fancy. Treat it as the supply point, not a destination meal.
food_drink.where: Collingwood, Golden Bay, on the way to and from Wharariki Road.

food_drink.name: Takaka, the Golden Bay hub
food_drink.description: Takaka is the main town of Golden Bay and the broadest choice of food in the area, roughly 40 km from the beach by road. It's the realistic base for a Wharariki day trip and where most visitors eat properly before driving out to the coast.
food_drink.where: Takaka, about 40 km from Wharariki Beach (nearest town in the beach data).

timeline.title: Cook names Cape Farewell
timeline.description: James Cook in HMS Endeavour named Cape Farewell, the northernmost point of the South Island, in March 1770 as he left New Zealand. Wharariki Beach lies on the Tasman Sea coast just west of the cape.

timeline.title: Marine Mammals Protection Act
timeline.description: New Zealand's Marine Mammals Protection Act 1978 makes it an offence to harass seals. It is the legal basis for the Department of Conservation's request that visitors keep at least 20 metres from the fur seals and their pups at Wharariki.

timeline.title: Windows 10 ships with the Archway Islands lock screen
timeline.description: Microsoft released Windows 10 in July 2015 with a photograph of Wharariki Beach's Archway Islands, shot from a cave on the beach, as its default lock screen image — making this corner of Golden Bay one of the most-viewed coastal scenes in the world.

timeline.title: The cave wallpaper becomes a meme
timeline.description: In the years after Windows 10's launch the Wharariki cave lock screen became a recurring internet meme and 'where is this?' curiosity, much as the Windows XP 'Bliss' hill had before it, driving travellers to identify and seek out the real beach.
timeline.source: Know Your Meme: Windows 10 Wharariki Beach Cave Wallpaper

timeline.title: Kaikoura-quake-era seal recovery context
timeline.description: New Zealand fur seal numbers, devastated by 19th-century sealing, had recovered strongly by the 2010s, and colonies like Wharariki's became reliable summer pup-watching sites — the basis for the beach's wildlife appeal alongside the arches.
timeline.source: Wikipedia: New Zealand fur seal; DOC

timeline.title: Windows 11 arrives; the Windows 10 image keeps drawing visitors
timeline.description: Microsoft released Windows 11 in 2021 with new default imagery, but the Wharariki Archway Islands shot remained firmly associated with Windows 10 and continued to send 'find the wallpaper' travellers to Golden Bay.

timeline.title: Microsoft ends mainstream Windows 10 support
timeline.description: Microsoft set the end of standard Windows 10 support for October 2025, closing the decade in which Wharariki's arches were the operating system's lock screen — by which point the once-anonymous beach was a known travel destination in its own right.

zones.name: The Archway Islands flats
zones.character: The open sand flats facing the four Archway Islands, the sea stacks eroded into arches that run from the beach edge at low tide out to about 250 metres offshore. At low water you can walk out across the wet sand toward the nearest stacks; this is the view that became the Windows 10 lock screen.
zones.best_for: Walking the flats and photographing the arches at low tide
zones.notes: Time it to low tide — at high water the flats are gone and the beach narrows back against the cliffs.

zones.name: The eastern seal pools
zones.character: The rock pools in front of the easternmost of the Archway Islands, where New Zealand fur seal pups gather and play in summer. Reachable on foot at low tide; the reliable spot to see the pups.
zones.best_for: Watching fur seal pups (from a distance)
zones.notes: Keep at least 20 m back and do not follow or chase the seals — harassment is an offence under the Marine Mammals Protection Act 1978. Keep dogs and small children clear.

zones.name: The backing dunes
zones.character: Large grassy dunes behind the beach, described as quite exposed to winds. The marram and coastal grasses here are what bend and stream in the famous wind photographs; this is the windward buffer between the farm track and the sand.
zones.best_for: Shelter from the worst of the wind; the descent point onto the beach
zones.notes: Stay on the track where marked to avoid trampling the dune vegetation that holds the sand together.

zones.name: Car park and farm track
zones.character: The end of Wharariki Road, about 4 km from Puponga, where the car park and the ~20-minute walking track begin. The path crosses Puponga Farm Park paddocks and a band of coastal forest before dropping to the beach.
zones.best_for: Parking and starting the walk in
zones.notes: There is no road to the sand and no services on the beach — bring water and carry out all rubbish.

landmarks.name: The Archway Islands
landmarks.description: A group of four islands and sea stacks off Wharariki Beach, eroded by the Tasman swell into the arches they are named for, ranging from part of the beach at low tide to roughly 250 metres offshore. Photographed from a cave on the beach, they are the default Windows 10 lock screen image.

landmarks.name: Cape Farewell
landmarks.description: The northernmost point of New Zealand's South Island, named by James Cook in 1770, immediately east of Wharariki Beach. The headland marks the western end of Farewell Spit and anchors this remote corner of Golden Bay.

landmarks.name: Farewell Spit
landmarks.description: The long curving sandspit and nature reserve east of Cape Farewell, a Ramsar wetland of international importance for birds and a frequent site of whale strandings. It shares the Puponga Farm Park gateway with the Wharariki Beach track.

cultural_refs.title: Windows 10 default lock screen
cultural_refs.creator: Microsoft
cultural_refs.description: The Archway Islands off Wharariki Beach, shot from a cave on the sand, are the default lock screen image of Microsoft's Windows 10 (released 2015) — making this remote Golden Bay beach one of the most-viewed coastal images in the world.

cultural_refs.title: Wharariki cave wallpaper (Steve McCurry, attributed)
cultural_refs.creator: Steve McCurry (attributed)
cultural_refs.description: The Windows 10 lock screen photograph is widely attributed to photographer Steve McCurry across secondary sources, showing the Archway Islands from a beach cave. Wikipedia names the operating system but not the photographer, so the credit is well-reported rather than primary-sourced.

cultural_refs.title: Windows 10 Wharariki Beach Cave Wallpaper (meme)
cultural_refs.description: After Windows 10's launch the cave lock screen became a recurring internet meme and 'where is this real place?' curiosity, echoing the earlier fame of the Windows XP 'Bliss' hill and helping turn an anonymous beach into a travel destination.

cultural_refs.title: Wharariki — Phormium cookianum (mountain flax)
cultural_refs.description: The beach's Maori name, wharariki, is the word for Phormium cookianum, the mountain or coastal flax native to New Zealand's exposed cliffs and coasts — a taonga (treasured) plant traditionally used for weaving and fibre.

recurring_events.name: Summer fur seal pup season
recurring_events.description: Through the warmer months New Zealand fur seal pups gather and play in the rock pools in front of the easternmost Archway Island, the main reason many visitors time a Wharariki walk for summer and low tide. Keep at least 20 metres back.

recurring_events.name: Twice-daily low-tide window
recurring_events.description: Wharariki is a low-tide beach: at low water the sand flats open, the rock pools drain, and you can walk out toward the Archway stacks and the seal pools. At high tide much of that ground is underwater, so visitors plan around the tide table, not opening hours.

things_to_know.label: It's a walk, not a drive
things_to_know.note: No road reaches the sand. Park at the end of Wharariki Road (about 4 km past Puponga) and allow ~20 minutes each way on a track through farm paddocks and coastal forest. Wear real shoes and bring water; there are no services on the beach.

things_to_know.label: Do not swim
things_to_know.note: The Department of Conservation says there are 'strong currents and rips at Wharariki Beach, swimming not recommended.' It's an unpatrolled, exposed, cold Tasman Sea coast. Come to walk, watch seals and photograph the arches — not to swim.

things_to_know.label: Time it to low tide
things_to_know.note: At low tide the flats open up, the rock pools drain, and you can walk out toward the Archway Islands and the seal pools. At high tide much of that is underwater. Check the day's low-tide time before you set out.

things_to_know.label: Keep your distance from the seals
things_to_know.note: Stay at least 20 metres from the fur seals and pups, don't follow or chase them, and keep dogs and small children well clear. Harassing a seal is an offence under the Marine Mammals Protection Act 1978; pups are curious and may approach you, so the distance is yours to keep.

access_note: Wharariki Beach is at the far northwest tip of the South Island, in Golden Bay (Tasman District), west of Cape Farewell. From Takaka, the main Golden Bay town about 40 km away, drive through Collingwood to Puponga, then take Wharariki Road to the car park at its end (about 4 km from Puponga). From the car park it is a roughly 20-minute walk over Puponga Farm Park paddocks and through coastal forest to the beach; there is no vehicle access to the sand and no services on it. Time your visit around low tide for the flats and the seal pools, and bring water and warm, windproof layers.
